story:
High in Cloudbreaker's forbidden glade, where some snow never melts, Fenric waited for the ermine-flash of Isenra among the emerald trees. He kept an exclamation's distance from the village that had branded his family cursed for guiding explorers up the mountain fifteen years before. Halmar—father by name, gaoler in truth—had forbidden him to leave that morning; Fenric ran anyway. He had only two freedoms: the herb-gathering excuse and the path nobody walked.

Isenra came, climbing lightly, hair a chestnut eddy, arms full of mint, thyme, sorrel. Their mouths struck like kindling; then her question: How long today? His half-truth: Maybe forever. When she drew back, he told her—he'd fought Halmar, defied the lock and key the past kept at Halmar's belt. If he returned, he might not be let out again.

They had met months ago when a laugh from a tree made him drop his sage and his guard. She loved the wild and loved him against all sense. In secret they had narrowed the distance between their "kingdoms" to nothing. Now fear edged her voice: Did he tell Halmar about her? No. Did Halmar follow? No—he would not tread the cursed path to the summit. Isenra, who heard what people said beyond Fenric's door, knew the village had once climbed after the explorers and left what they found where it lay.

A stumble broke the spell. Fenric toppled a cairn of stones and uncovered a rusted tin stamped with foreign script. He opened it. Two oilcloth bundles. The first, sealed in wax with a noble profile, held a letter in the lingua that bound Cloudbreaker to distant lands. He read aloud:

March 25th, 1844. We are camped at the last place on Cloudbreaker where life is possible. Twelve men, joined by two guides—a man and woman who know the tongues, the plants, the waters. Halmar is stalwart, upright. Vittris is possessed of an uncommon knowledge of the natural world. Her intellect and beauty surpass any I have encountered. All is well... Expedition Leader, Thoren Alvar.

Fenric knew the facts, but not this admiring hand. The village version, Isenra said softly, claimed Thoren had used Vittris. The letter complicated that cruelty without settling it. Fenric counted months from his birth to the expedition's date. The pieces set and shattered in the same breath. He was Thoren's. Perhaps that was the root of Halmar's hate.

He opened the second note, the ink frailer:

April 30th, 1844. Unexpected snowfall cut us off before summit. Betrayed by deep winter. Pass blocked. Vittris and I sent Halmar down for help; he alone might make it through the lower route. The mountain offers no sustenance. We cannot hold much longer. Gods forgive us. Expd. Leader Thoren Alvar.

Fenric's breath smoked. Halmar had returned too late—or not at all. Not all twelve had died waiting, but many had. The plea for forgiveness darkened the air. Isenra's calm: They were desperate. Do you understand what that means? He refused the thought, wanted to bury the tin, the letters, the man who might be his father.

What now? he asked. Are you horrified by me? She lifted her hand as if stilling the question itself. I always knew, she said, touching his arm. Most people believe some version of this; the truth is… fascinating. She kissed him, eyes bright as lichen—and harder. After this, go home. Apologise to Halmar. The villagers would do worse; I think Halmar has shielded you. I'll find a way for us to go on meeting.

Her fingers stroked behind his ear; her teeth grazed cartilage; her whisper was a tether and a warning. Snow began to drift. Down the slope, where he had first seen her ermine-bright, a shadow moved in the hunter-green dark.
